choices.text: Administering influenza vaccines at a local clinic.
Providing individual counseling to a patient with diabetes.
Teaching a group of parents about childhood immunizations.
Making a home visit to a patient recovering from surgery.

questions.rationale: Teaching a group promotes health to the community as a whole.  The other options address individual needs.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: For a client with Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), which assessment finding indicates effective treatment?
questions.rationale: The primary goal of DKA treatment is to correct hyperglycemia.  A decrease in blood glucose indicates effective treatment.  While increased urine output and decreased Kussmaul respirations are positive signs, they are secondary to the correction of hyperglycemia.  Decreased serum potassium is a concern due to potassium shifting from cells during treatment.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse just completed a colonoscopy with polypectomy.  Which postoperative assessments are crucial?

choices.text: Assess for signs of perforation (e.g., abdominal rigidity, fever).

questions.rationale: Post-polypectomy, bleeding is a major concern.  Vital signs monitor for shock.  Abdominal distention and absent bowel sounds can indicate perforation or bowel obstruction.  Electrolyte imbalances can result from fluid loss related to bleeding or bowel perforation.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient receiving chemotherapy reports mouth sores. What is the appropriate nursing intervention?

choices.text: Encourage frequent mouth rinsing with an alcohol-based mouthwash.
Provide the patient with hard candies to suck on.
Encourage frequent mouth rinsing with saline solution.

questions.rationale: Saline rinses help clean and soothe the mouth.  Alcohol-based mouthwashes are drying and irritating.  Hard candies and acidic juices can worsen the sores.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with pneumonia is exhibiting signs of respiratory distress. Which assessment finding requires immediate attention?

choices.text: Oxygen saturation of 88% on room air.

questions.rationale: An oxygen saturation of 88% indicates hypoxemia, a life-threatening condition that requires immediate intervention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving both digoxin and spironolactone. What should the nurse monitor closely?
questions.rationale: Spironolactone is a potassium-sparing diuretic, and digoxin toxicity is exacerbated by hyperkalemia.  Therefore, close monitoring of potassium levels is crucial.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with acute pancreatitis is experiencing severe abdominal pain.  Which nursing interventions are appropriate?

choices.text: Place the patient in a semi-Fowler's position.

questions.rationale: Semi-Fowler's position helps reduce pain.  Analgesics manage pain.  Hypocalcemia is a complication of pancreatitis.  NPO status reduces pancreatic stimulation.  Mouth care addresses dry mouth from NPO status. Ambulation is contraindicated due to pain.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A 6-month-old infant presents with dehydration.  Which sign is the most concerning?
questions.rationale: Sunken fontanelles indicate severe dehydration and require immediate intervention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which lab value is most concerning for a patient with chronic kidney disease (CKD)?
questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia is a serious complication of CKD that can lead to cardiac arrhythmias.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with schizophrenia is experiencing auditory hallucinations.  What is a therapeutic nursing intervention?

choices.text: Tell the patient the voices are not real.
Ask the patient to describe the voices.
Ignore the patient's statements about the voices.

questions.rationale: Understanding the content of the hallucinations helps the nurse assess the patient's needs and develop appropriate interventions.  Directly contradicting the patient is unhelpful.  Ignoring the patient invalidates their experience.  Medication is important but not the immediate priority over assessment and therapeutic communication.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A 2-year-old child presents with a barking cough and stridor. What is the nurse's priority concern?
questions.rationale: A barking cough and stridor are classic symptoms of croup, which is characterized by upper airway obstruction. Maintaining a patent airway is the immediate priority. Dehydration, pneumonia, and otitis media are important considerations, but airway patency is paramount in this scenario.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient diagnosed with schizophrenia is exhibiting disorganized thought processes and auditory hallucinations. Which nursing intervention is MOST important?

choices.text: Encourage the patient to participate in group therapy.
Establish a therapeutic relationship built on trust and rapport.
Redirect the patient to reality-oriented activities.

questions.rationale: While medication (B), group therapy (A), and reality orientation (D) are all important aspects of managing schizophrenia, establishing a therapeutic relationship (C) is paramount.  Building trust and rapport provides a foundation for effective communication and intervention, allowing the nurse to better assess the patient's needs and address their symptoms more effectively. The disorganized thought processes and hallucinations make other interventions less effective unless the nurse first builds rapport and trust.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Potassium level is 5.8 mEq/L. Which nursing intervention is the priority?
questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia (potassium level > 5.0 mEq/L) can lead to life-threatening cardiac dysrhythmias.  Therefore, continuous cardiac monitoring (C) is the priority to detect and manage potential arrhythmias.  While dietary restriction (A), Kayexalate administration (B), and increased fluid intake (D) are all appropriate interventions for hyperkalemia, cardiac monitoring takes precedence to prevent potentially fatal complications.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: For a client with Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), which assessment findings require immediate nursing intervention? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: DKA is a serious complication of diabetes characterized by hyperglycemia, ketosis, and acidosis. While high blood glucose (A), Kussmaul respirations (B), and low bicarbonate (F) are characteristic of DKA, they are not the most immediately life-threatening.  Hypokalemia (C) is a common finding in DKA and can lead to serious cardiac complications, requiring immediate intervention. Hypotension (D) indicates poor perfusion and requires prompt attention.  Low urine output (E), while concerning, is not as urgent as the electrolyte imbalance and hemodynamic instability.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action best demonstrates a nurse's role in health promotion?

choices.text: Educating a community group on healthy lifestyle choices.

questions.rationale: Health promotion focuses on preventing disease and improving overall well-being. Educating a community group on healthy lifestyle choices directly addresses this goal. Administering vaccinations (A), providing wound care (B), and monitoring vital signs (D) are all important nursing actions but are more focused on treatment and assessment than on health promotion.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A pregnant woman at 36 weeks gestation reports sudden onset of severe abdominal pain.  What is the nurse's priority action?
questions.rationale: Sudden severe abdominal pain at 36 weeks gestation could indicate a serious complication such as placental abruption or uterine rupture.  Prompt notification of the physician (D) is crucial to allow for immediate assessment and intervention.  While monitoring fetal heart rate (A), assessing for vaginal bleeding (B), and administering oxygen (C) are important, notifying the physician is the priority action to ensure timely intervention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a nasogastric (NG) tube in place reports nausea and abdominal distention. Which actions should the nurse take? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Irrigate the NG tube with normal saline.
Elevate the head of the bed.

questions.rationale: Nausea and abdominal distention with an NG tube in place could indicate several problems, including incorrect tube placement (A), bowel obstruction, or excessive residual volume.  Checking tube placement (A) is crucial to ensure the NG tube is in the correct location.  Assessing bowel sounds (C) helps determine the presence of any bowel obstruction.  Elevating the head of the bed (E) can help alleviate nausea and promote gastric emptying.  Checking for residual volume (F) helps determine if the stomach is overly full, contributing to the distention.  Irrigating the NG tube (B) should only be done if there is a blockage confirmed by other assessments.  Antiemetics (D) might be given if nausea is persistent but are not addressing the underlying cause.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient receiving chemotherapy reports severe mucositis. Which intervention is the priority?
questions.rationale: Mucositis is inflammation of the mucous membranes and is a common side effect of chemotherapy.  Frequent oral hygiene (D) is crucial to prevent infection and promote healing. While the other options are also important, oral hygiene is the most direct intervention to address the primary problem of mucositis.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with pneumonia exhibits increased shortness of breath and decreased oxygen saturation. Which interventions should the nurse implement? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Encourage deep breathing and coughing exercises.
Elevate the head of the bed.
Restrict fluids to prevent fluid overload.

questions.rationale: Increased shortness of breath and decreased oxygen saturation in a patient with pneumonia indicate worsening respiratory status.  Administering oxygen therapy (A) is crucial to improve oxygenation.  Assessing lung sounds (B) helps evaluate the extent of lung involvement.  Deep breathing and coughing exercises (C) help clear secretions and improve ventilation.  Elevating the head of the bed (D) facilitates breathing.  Frequent monitoring of vital signs (E) is essential to assess the patient's response to interventions.  Fluid restriction (F) is generally not recommended for patients with pneumonia unless there is evidence of fluid overload; adequate hydration is essential for effective mucus clearance.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient taking warfarin has an INR of 8.0.  What is the priority nursing action?

choices.text: Hold the next dose of warfarin.

questions.rationale: An INR of 8.0 indicates a significantly elevated risk of bleeding.  Assessing for signs of bleeding (B) is the priority action to identify and manage potential complications.  While vitamin K (A) may be administered, it is not the immediate priority; the focus is on identifying and addressing any existing bleeding. Holding warfarin (C) is also appropriate, but assessing for bleeding is the first step. Increasing fluids (D) might be helpful in some cases but doesn't address the immediate risk of bleeding.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with pancreatitis reports severe epigastric pain. Which nursing intervention will best manage the pain?

choices.text: Position the patient in a side-lying position.
Apply a heating pad to the abdomen.

questions.rationale: Positioning the patient in a side-lying position (B) with knees flexed often provides pain relief for patients with pancreatitis.  NSAIDs (A) are generally avoided in pancreatitis due to their potential to increase inflammation.  Heat (C) may increase inflammation and is usually avoided.  Ambulation (D) may be encouraged once pain is controlled, but pain management is the priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with chronic kidney disease (CKD) has a BUN of 100 mg/dL and creatinine of 6.0 mg/dL. Which nursing assessment is MOST important?
questions.rationale: Elevated BUN and creatinine levels indicate severe kidney failure and accumulation of waste products in the body.  Neurological assessment (C) is crucial to detect signs of uremic encephalopathy, a potentially life-threatening complication.  While daily weights (A), I&O (B), and skin turgor (D) are important, neurological status takes precedence.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A 6-month-old infant presents with fever, diarrhea, and dehydration.  What is the nurse’s priority intervention?
questions.rationale: Fever, diarrhea, and dehydration in an infant can lead to severe dehydration and shock. Monitoring for signs of shock (C) (tachycardia, hypotension, altered mental status) is the priority to prevent a life-threatening emergency. While the other interventions are appropriate, preventing shock is paramount.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with hypothyroidism is started on levothyroxine.  What should the nurse teach the patient about this medication?

choices.text: Take the medication with food to improve absorption.
Take the medication at bedtime to minimize side effects.
The medication may cause weight gain.
The medication may take several weeks to show full effects.

questions.rationale: Levothyroxine takes several weeks to reach therapeutic levels.  It's best taken on an empty stomach for optimal absorption.  It usually causes weight loss and may cause insomnia if taken at bedtime.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with depression states, "I feel hopeless and worthless.  Life isn't worth living anymore." Which nursing interventions are appropriate? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Encourage the patient to participate in group therapy.
Leave the patient alone to allow for introspection.

questions.rationale: The patient's statements indicate a high risk for suicide.  Assessing for suicidal ideation (A) is crucial to develop a safety plan.  Encouraging group therapy (B) and administering antidepressants (C) are appropriate interventions to address the depression.  Close monitoring (D) is essential to prevent self-harm.  Accurate documentation (F) is essential for legal and medical reasons.  Leaving the patient alone (E) is unsafe and inappropriate.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: An elderly patient is exhibiting signs of delirium. Which factors could be contributing to this condition? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Delirium in older adults is often multifactorial.  Dehydration (A), infection (B), medication side effects (C), electrolyte imbalance (D), and lack of sleep (E) are all common contributing factors.  The normal aging process (F) does not directly cause delirium, though age-related changes can increase vulnerability.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A 2-year-old child is admitted with suspected bacterial meningitis.  Which assessment findings would the nurse anticipate?
questions.rationale: Bacterial meningitis in young children often presents with a combination of symptoms including high fever (A), nuchal rigidity (B), lethargy (C), bulging fontanelle (D), positive Brudzinski's sign (E), and decreased appetite (F). All these are classic signs and warrant immediate medical attention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: An elderly patient with dementia is refusing to take their medications. What is the best approach?

choices.text: Force the patient to take the medication.
Offer the medication with a favorite snack or beverage.
Explain the importance of the medication in detail.
Ignore the patient's refusal and try again later.

questions.rationale: Elderly patients with dementia may have difficulty understanding instructions or complying with medication regimens. Offering the medication with a preferred food or beverage increases the likelihood of compliance without resorting to force or coercion.  Explaining the medication in detail is unlikely to be effective due to cognitive impairment. Ignoring the refusal is not appropriate and could delay necessary treatment.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: After administering furosemide (Lasix), which finding would require immediate intervention?
questions.rationale: Furosemide is a potent loop diuretic that can cause significant potassium loss (hypokalemia). A serum potassium level of 2.8 mEq/L is dangerously low and can lead to life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias.  While increased urine output, weight loss, and decreased blood pressure are expected effects of furosemide, hypokalemia is a serious adverse effect that requires immediate intervention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with chronic kidney disease (CKD) is experiencing worsening edema.  Which nursing interventions are appropriate?
questions.rationale: Managing edema in CKD involves several interventions. Monitoring daily weight (A) helps track fluid balance. Fluid restriction (B) is often necessary to reduce edema. Elevating the legs (C) promotes venous return. Diuretics (D) help remove excess fluid.  Assessing for infection (E) is crucial as infections can worsen fluid retention.  Protein intake should be carefully monitored based on kidney function (F is incorrect, as protein restriction is often necessary).

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: "My chest feels tight, and I can't breathe," a patient anxiously states. What is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Instruct the patient in deep breathing exercises.
Assess the patient's oxygen saturation and respiratory rate.
Immediately administer a prescribed anxiolytic medication.
Reassure the patient that everything will be alright.

questions.rationale: The patient's statement indicates potential respiratory distress. The nurse's priority is to assess the patient's respiratory status by checking oxygen saturation and respiratory rate before initiating other interventions. While deep breathing exercises, anxiolytics, and reassurance may be appropriate depending on the assessment findings, accurate assessment of the patient's respiratory status comes first.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse just completed a dressing change for a patient with a draining wound.  What should the nurse do with the soiled dressing?

choices.text: Place it in a regular trash can.
Dispose of it in a biohazard waste container.
Wrap it in a plastic bag and place it in the linen hamper.

questions.rationale: Soiled dressings from draining wounds are considered infectious waste and must be disposed of in a biohazard waste container to prevent the spread of infection.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A child with cystic fibrosis presents with increased cough and thick mucus. Select all appropriate nursing interventions.
questions.rationale: All options are appropriate interventions for a child with cystic fibrosis experiencing an exacerbation. Chest physiotherapy (A) helps to loosen and remove mucus. Increased fluid intake (B) helps to thin the mucus. Monitoring oxygen saturation (C) assesses respiratory status. Rest periods (D) are crucial to conserve energy. Antibiotics (E) are often prescribed to manage infections. Nutritional support (F) is essential due to the malabsorption issues associated with cystic fibrosis.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: After administering furosemide, which electrolyte imbalance should the nurse monitor for?
questions.rationale: Furosemide is a potent loop diuretic that causes significant potassium loss, leading to hypokalemia.  The other options are not the primary electrolyte imbalance associated with furosemide use.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is prescribed digoxin 0.125 mg daily. The medication is available in 0.25 mg tablets. How many tablets should the nurse administer?
questions.rationale: 0.125 mg / 0.25 mg/tablet = 0.5 tablets.  The nurse would administer half a tablet.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is experiencing increased shortness of breath and wheezing.  Which assessment finding would require immediate attention?

choices.text: Use of accessory muscles for breathing

questions.rationale: An oxygen saturation of 88% indicates hypoxemia, a life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention.  The other findings are concerning but not as immediately life-threatening.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with type 1 diabetes is exhibiting signs of hypoglycemia.  Which of the following are appropriate nursing interventions?

choices.text: Offer the patient 15 grams of carbohydrates.
Check for ketones in the urine.

questions.rationale: Administering rapid-acting insulin would worsen hypoglycemia.  Checking for ketones is relevant for hyperglycemia, not hypoglycemia.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with severe dehydration would likely exhibit which of the following symptoms?
questions.rationale: Dehydration leads to decreased blood volume, resulting in hypotension.  Bradycardia, edema, and bounding pulses are not associated with dehydration.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which of the following is the best method for cleaning a wound?
questions.rationale: Sterile saline solution is the most appropriate solution for wound cleaning to minimize tissue damage and infection risk.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a nasogastric (NG) tube is experiencing nausea and vomiting.  What is the priority nursing action?

choices.text: Increase the rate of NG tube feeding.
Check the placement of the NG tube.
Administer an antiemetic medication as ordered.
Encourage the patient to drink clear liquids.

questions.rationale: Checking NG tube placement is the priority to ensure that the feeding is not entering the lungs.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving oxygen via a nasal cannula.  What is the maximum flow rate typically used with a nasal cannula?
questions.rationale: Higher flow rates with nasal cannulas can cause nasal dryness and irritation, and may not increase oxygen saturation significantly.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A priority assessment for a postoperative patient is:
questions.rationale: All three are crucial for postoperative patient safety and recovery.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: What is the best way to clean a new wound?

choices.text: Leave the wound uncovered to air dry.

questions.rationale: Soap and water are the best way to clean a new wound to minimize infection.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Signs and symptoms of hyperthyroidism include:
questions.rationale: Hyperthyroidism causes increased metabolism, leading to weight loss, heat intolerance, and tremors.  A goiter may also be present.  Bradycardia and constipation are associated with hypothyroidism.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a urinary catheter should have their intake and output monitored closely.  Why is this important?
questions.rationale: Accurate I&O monitoring is crucial for all three reasons.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is experiencing significant anxiety related to a recent diagnosis.  Which nursing intervention demonstrates a holistic approach to care?

choices.text: Facilitating communication with family and support systems.

questions.rationale: A holistic approach involves addressing the patient's physical, emotional, and social needs.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task can a nurse safely delegate to a certified nursing assistant (CNA)?
questions.rationale: CNAs can take vital signs; medication administration, pain assessment, and sterile dressing changes require nursing judgment and skills.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient refuses a necessary treatment.  What is the nurse's ethical responsibility?

choices.text: Convince the patient to receive the treatment.
Respect the patient's autonomy and document the refusal.
Contact the physician to override the patient’s decision.
Tell the patient they must receive the treatment.

questions.rationale: Respecting patient autonomy is paramount.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Following the administration of a newly prescribed beta-blocker, which assessment finding requires immediate intervention?
questions.rationale: A heart rate of 52 bpm is bradycardia, a potentially serious side effect of beta-blockers that requires immediate intervention.  The other options represent common, less serious side effects.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient newly diagnosed with diabetes mellitus needs education on blood glucose monitoring. What should be taught first?

choices.text: How to perform the finger stick
What to do if the glucose is high

questions.rationale: Before a patient can interpret results or manage high glucose, they must first learn the basic procedure of performing the finger stick test.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse is caring for four patients. Which patient requires the most immediate attention?

choices.text: Patient with stable angina reporting chest pain
Patient post-op day 2 with a slightly elevated temperature
Patient with a new onset of shortness of breath and wheezing
Patient with a urinary tract infection complaining of burning on urination

questions.rationale: Shortness of breath and wheezing indicate a potential respiratory emergency requiring immediate intervention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: An LPN/LVN is assigned to a patient with a new ileostomy.  Which task is appropriate to delegate?

choices.text: Assessing the stoma's appearance and output
Teaching the patient about stoma care
Measuring the stoma's size and documenting findings

questions.rationale: Measuring and documenting are tasks within the scope of an LPN/LVN.  Assessment, teaching, and complex procedures like pouch changes require the RN's expertise.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient collapses in the hallway and is unresponsive. What is the first action?

choices.text: Check for a medical alert bracelet

questions.rationale: While assessment is important, immediately calling for a Code Blue is critical to initiate a rapid response team and ensure timely advanced care.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient refuses a life-sustaining treatment.  What is the nurse's primary ethical responsibility?

choices.text: Persuade the patient to accept treatment

questions.rationale: Respecting the patient's right to make decisions about their own healthcare is paramount.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with pneumonia is experiencing increased shortness of breath. Which nursing action should be taken first?

choices.text: Elevate the head of the bed

questions.rationale: Elevating the head of the bed improves ventilation and breathing. While the other options are necessary, elevating the head of the bed provides immediate respiratory relief.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A potassium level of 6.5 mEq/L indicates:
questions.rationale: A potassium level of 6.5 mEq/L is significantly above the normal range, indicating hyperkalemia.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: What discharge instructions should be given to a patient after a tonsillectomy?
questions.rationale: All options are appropriate discharge instructions after a tonsillectomy to prevent complications and promote healing.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: After providing wound care, what should the nurse do with used dressings?

choices.text: Place them in a regular trash can.
Dispose of them in a biohazard waste container.
Place them in a linen hamper.
Hand them to the housekeeping staff.

questions.rationale: Used dressings are considered contaminated and must be disposed of in a biohazard waste container.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task can a certified nursing assistant (CNA) safely perform for a patient with a urinary catheter?
questions.rationale: Emptying the drainage bag is a routine task within the CNA's scope of practice.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse witnesses a colleague making a medication error. What is the most appropriate action?

choices.text: Ignore it and hope it doesn't happen again.
Report it to the supervisor immediately.
Discuss it privately with the colleague.

questions.rationale: Reporting to the supervisor ensures patient safety and proper follow-up.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient's blood glucose is 250 mg/dL. What is the most likely cause?
questions.rationale: A blood glucose of 250 mg/dL indicates hyperglycemia.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient experiencing anaphylaxis requires which immediate interventions?
questions.rationale: All options represent crucial interventions in anaphylaxis management.  While antihistamines may be used later, epinephrine is the primary treatment for anaphylaxis.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Following administration of morphine sulfate 2mg IVP for pain, the patient's respiratory rate drops to 8 breaths per minute. What is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Document the finding and continue monitoring.
Increase the morphine dose to achieve better pain control.
Reassess the patient's pain level in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Respiratory depression is a serious side effect of opioid analgesics. Naloxone is an opioid antagonist that reverses the effects of opioids, including respiratory depression.  Options A, C, and D are incorrect and could worsen the patient's condition.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with pneumonia exhibits increased respiratory rate, shortness of breath, and decreased oxygen saturation.  Which nursing intervention is PRIORITY?
questions.rationale: The patient's priority need is oxygenation, given the decreased saturation and shortness of breath.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A newly admitted patient requires a complete bed bath.  To whom can the nurse safely delegate this task?
questions.rationale: UAPs are trained to perform basic hygiene tasks like bed baths.  Delegation to other healthcare professionals is inappropriate for this task. Family members can assist, but the nurse maintains responsibility for assessment and follow-up.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient refuses a necessary blood transfusion due to religious beliefs, despite the risk of death.  What is the nurse's ethical responsibility?

choices.text: Administer the blood transfusion regardless of the patient's wishes.
Respect the patient's autonomy and advocate for their choice.
Contact the patient's family to override their decision.
Inform the patient that they are making a mistake.

questions.rationale: Nurses must respect patient autonomy and advocate for their right to make informed decisions about their care, even if those decisions pose a risk.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient suddenly collapses and becomes unresponsive. What is the first action the nurse should take?
questions.rationale: Assessing responsiveness (e.g., "Are you okay?") is the first step in the emergency response sequence.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a head injury exhibits unilateral pupil dilation. What is the nurse's priority action?
questions.rationale: Unilateral pupil dilation can indicate increased intracranial pressure and requires immediate medical attention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a bowel obstruction reports abdominal distention and severe pain.  What is the most important nursing assessment?
questions.rationale: Assessing bowel sounds helps to determine the severity and location of the obstruction.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient taking lithium carbonate for bipolar disorder should be monitored for which side effects?
questions.rationale: Tremors, polyuria (increased urination), and increased thirst are common side effects of lithium.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Before discharging a patient home after surgery, what is the nurse's priority?

choices.text: Ensure the patient understands discharge instructions.

questions.rationale: Patient education is crucial to ensure safe transition to home.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient's blood glucose level is 30 mg/dL.  What is the nurse's first action?
questions.rationale: This is severe hypoglycemia and requires immediate treatment with a simple carbohydrate like orange juice.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A terminally ill patient requests assistance in ending their life.  How should the nurse respond?

choices.text: Provide the patient with the necessary means.
Contact the patient's family to discuss the situation.
Offer emotional support and explore the patient's concerns.

questions.rationale: The nurse's role is to provide emotional support and explore the patient's concerns while upholding ethical and legal boundaries.  Assisted suicide is illegal in most jurisdictions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Principles of surgical asepsis include:

choices.text: Touching only sterile items within the sterile field.

questions.rationale: All options are fundamental principles of surgical asepsis.  Handwashing is part of both medical and surgical asepsis.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Following administration of morphine sulfate 2mg IV, the patient's respiratory rate drops to 8 breaths per minute.  What is the priority nursing action?

choices.text: Document the finding and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Respiratory depression is a serious side effect of opioid analgesics. Naloxone is an opioid antagonist that reverses the effects of opioid overdose.  Options B, C, and D are inappropriate responses to respiratory depression.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient newly diagnosed with type 2 diabetes is being discharged. What essential teaching point must be emphasized?

choices.text: The importance of daily insulin injections.
The need for regular blood glucose monitoring.
The necessity of a strict, no-carbohydrate diet.
The use of an insulin pump for glucose control.

questions.rationale: Regular blood glucose monitoring is crucial for managing type 2 diabetes, allowing for adjustments to diet and medication as needed.  Insulin injections and insulin pumps are not always necessary for type 2 diabetes management, and a strict no-carbohydrate diet is unrealistic and unnecessary.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient's potassium level is 6.2 mEq/L. What nursing intervention is the priority?
questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia (high potassium) can cause serious cardiac complications.  Cardiac monitoring is the priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A licensed practical nurse (LPN) is assigned to your patient care unit.  Which task is appropriate to delegate to the LPN?

choices.text: Assessing a patient's neurological status post-stroke.
Monitoring a patient's post-operative vital signs and intake/output.

questions.rationale: Monitoring vital signs and I&O is within the scope of practice for an LPN.  Administering blood transfusions, assessing neurological status, and changing central line dressings require the skills and judgment of an RN.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: You have four patients. Which patient requires immediate attention?

choices.text: A patient with stable angina experiencing chest discomfort.
A patient with a new onset of shortness of breath and oxygen saturation of 88%.
A patient postoperative day 2 complaining of incisional pain.
A patient with a urinary tract infection who needs antibiotics.

questions.rationale: The patient with acute shortness of breath and low oxygen saturation needs immediate attention due to potential respiratory compromise.  The other patients need attention, but their conditions are not immediately life-threatening.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient refuses a life-sustaining treatment, despite the family's wishes to continue treatment. Which ethical principle is primarily involved?
questions.rationale: Autonomy respects the patient's right to make decisions about their own body and medical care, even if those decisions differ from the family's wishes.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Post-operative pain management involves which primary nursing intervention?
questions.rationale: Post-operative pain management focuses on administering analgesics as prescribed to control pain and promote comfort.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: An Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP) is assisting with patient care.  Which task should NOT be delegated to the UAP?
questions.rationale: Assessing pain level requires nursing judgment and is beyond the scope of a UAP.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse is caring for multiple patients. Which patient needs immediate attention?

choices.text: A patient who is two hours post-op and has a slightly elevated temperature.
A patient complaining of mild nausea.
A patient who is experiencing significant shortness of breath and wheezing.
A patient with a urinary tract infection who is awaiting antibiotics.

questions.rationale: Respiratory distress requires immediate intervention due to potential respiratory failure.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is experiencing a seizure. What is the priority nursing action?

choices.text: Place a tongue depressor in the patient's mouth.

questions.rationale: Protecting the patient from injury is the immediate priority during a seizure.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient has an indwelling urinary catheter. What is a crucial nursing intervention?

choices.text: Maintain a closed urinary drainage system.

questions.rationale: Maintaining a closed system prevents infection.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient's arterial blood gas (ABG) reveals respiratory acidosis.  Which is the most likely cause?
questions.rationale: Respiratory acidosis is caused by hypoventilation, leading to increased CO2 levels.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse witnesses a colleague documenting inaccurate information in a patient's chart. What is the appropriate action?

choices.text: Report the incident to the supervisor.
Document the incident in the colleague's personnel file.

questions.rationale: Reporting the incident to the supervisor is the ethical and professional action to take.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A 2-year-old presents with a barking cough, stridor, and increased respiratory effort.  Pulse oximetry shows 90% saturation on room air.

choices.text:
Obtain a chest x-ray.
Correct Answer: C, E, possibly B
Rationale:  The clinical presentation suggests croup (laryngotracheobronchitis). While albuterol might be used, the priority is to improve oxygenation given the low saturation.  Oxygen via nasal cannula should be started immediately.  A chest x-ray will help rule out other causes. Intubation might be necessary if the child's respiratory status deteriorates, but it is not the first step. Assessing for dehydration is important but less urgent than addressing the immediate respiratory distress.

--- End Question ---

Question Number: 3
Category: Gerontology
Type: Multiple Choice
Difficulty: Medium
Question: "I'm so dizzy and weak," states an 80-year-old patient with a history of atrial fibrillation.  Their heart rate is 160 bpm and irregular.
A)  Check the patient's potassium level.
B)  Administer digoxin as ordered.
C)  Assess for signs of dehydration.
D)  Immediately initiate cardioversion.

questions.rationale: The high heart rate and dizziness/weakness in a patient with atrial fibrillation suggest potential complications like dehydration or electrolyte imbalances (especially potassium).  Checking the potassium level is vital. Assessing for dehydration is also crucial given the patient's age.  Digoxin should not be administered without a thorough evaluation; cardioversion is a last resort.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Heparin sodium 5000 units subcutaneous is prescribed.  The vial is labeled 10,000 units/mL.
questions.rationale: To calculate the correct dose, divide the desired dose (5000 units) by the concentration (10,000 units/mL).  This results in 0.5 mL.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) is on mechanical ventilation.  Their arterial blood gas (ABG) shows a PaO2 of 50 mmHg and PaCO2 of 60 mmHg.  What is the priority action?

choices.text:
Increase the FiO2.
Correct Answer: E, possibly B
Rationale: The ABG indicates severe hypoxemia and hypercapnia.  Increasing the FiO2 (fraction of inspired oxygen) is the immediate priority to improve oxygenation. Increasing respiratory rate might also be considered if the PaCO2 remains high despite FiO2 adjustment. The other options are not the immediate priority in managing acute respiratory failure.

--- End Question ---

Question Number: 7
Category:  Neurology
Type: Multiple Choice
Difficulty: Medium
Question:  A patient with a new diagnosis of multiple sclerosis (MS) asks, "Will I ever walk again?"
A) "Most people with MS eventually lose their ability to walk."
B) "It's difficult to predict the course of your illness."
C) "You'll likely need a wheelchair within a year."
D)  "Focus on the positive; you can manage this."
E)  "Let's talk about the resources available to help you maintain your mobility."

questions.rationale: The nurse should offer empathy and support while providing realistic information. Option E provides a supportive and helpful response, focusing on available resources. The other options are overly pessimistic, dismissive, or offer false reassurance.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a nasogastric (NG) tube reports nausea and abdominal distention.

choices.text: Increase the rate of NG tube feeding.
Irrigate the NG tube with normal saline.

questions.rationale: Nausea and abdominal distention with an NG tube could indicate that the tube is misplaced, clogged or that the feeding is too rapid. Checking tube placement is crucial, and administering an antiemetic may relieve nausea. Irrigating the tube may help but is not the first action. Increasing feeding rate is contraindicated.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with type 1 diabetes is found unresponsive. Their blood glucose is 20 mg/dL.
questions.rationale: Severe hypoglycemia (20 mg/dL) requires immediate treatment with a rapid-acting carbohydrate source like 50% dextrose intravenously. Glucagon is an alternative if IV access is not immediately available.  Insulin is contraindicated. Orange juice is too slow to act.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with chronic kidney disease (CKD) reports increasing fatigue and shortness of breath.  Their hemoglobin is 8 g/dL.
questions.rationale: Low hemoglobin suggests anemia, a common complication of CKD. Erythropoietin stimulates red blood cell production.  Fluid restriction is typically used in CKD, not increased intake.  Protein restriction is also part of CKD management, but doesn't address the anemia directly.  Infection can worsen anemia but is not the direct cause in this scenario.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with schizophrenia is experiencing auditory hallucinations and exhibiting paranoia. They are becoming increasingly agitated.

choices.text: Engage the patient in a reality-oriented conversation.
Administer an antipsychotic medication as ordered.
Place the patient in seclusion immediately.
Ignore the patient's behavior to avoid escalation.

questions.rationale: Agitation in a patient with schizophrenia needs a multi-pronged approach.  Medication is necessary to manage the symptoms, but it's also crucial to use therapeutic communication techniques to try to de-escalate the situation. Seclusion should only be used as a last resort.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is receiving heparin. What is a priority assessment?

choices.text: Assess for pain at the insertion site.

questions.rationale: Heparin is an anticoagulant; therefore, monitoring for signs of bleeding (bruising, petechiae, epistaxis, hematuria) is a priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is prescribed a medication with a half-life of 4 hours.  What percentage of the medication will remain in their system after 8 hours?
questions.rationale: After one half-life (4 hours), 50% remains. After two half-lives (8 hours), 25% remains.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse is performing a home visit for a patient with newly diagnosed hypertension.  What is a priority assessment?
questions.rationale: Comprehensive assessment includes medication adherence, understanding of the condition, and the patient's social support which greatly impacts their ability to manage their health.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving oxygen via nasal cannula at 4L/min.  What is a priority assessment?
questions.rationale: All are important assessments for a patient on oxygen therapy.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with severe dehydration has dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor, and hypotension.  What is the priority intervention?
questions.rationale: Severe dehydration requires rapid fluid replacement, usually intravenously.  Oral rehydration is appropriate for mild dehydration.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with acute pancreatitis has severe abdominal pain, elevated amylase and lipase levels, and hypotension.  What is the priority nursing intervention?
questions.rationale: All interventions are crucial in managing acute pancreatitis. Pain management, NPO status to reduce pancreatic stimulation, and close monitoring for shock due to hypotension are essential.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving vancomycin.  What is a priority nursing intervention?
questions.rationale: Vancomycin can cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity. Monitoring for both is essential.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient post-abdominal surgery is complaining of severe incisional pain. What is the priority intervention?
questions.rationale: Pain management is the priority.  Other actions may be necessary after pain is addressed.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient at risk for falls is being discharged home.  Which discharge instructions are important?

choices.text: Keep frequently used items within reach.

questions.rationale: All except excessive alcohol consumption are important for fall prevention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient's wound is described as "red, warm, and tender to the touch."  What is suspected?
questions.rationale: These are signs of infection.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse is caring for several patients.  Which task can be delegated to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?

choices.text: Teaching a patient about their new medication.

questions.rationale: LPNs can change sterile dressings.  Assessment, teaching, and administering IV medications are typically within the scope of practice of RNs.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is being discharged on warfarin.  What is crucial patient education?
questions.rationale: All are important instructions for patients taking warfarin.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient expresses concern about their impending death.  What is the nurse's most appropriate action?
questions.rationale: Addressing spiritual needs provides comfort and support.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Potassium level is 5.8 mEq/L.  The nurse should prioritize which intervention?
questions.rationale: A potassium level of 5.8 mEq/L is hyperkalemia, a serious electrolyte imbalance that can cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias.  Therefore, continuous cardiac monitoring (B) is the priority.  While restricting potassium intake (C) and encouraging fluid intake (D) are appropriate, they are not as urgent as monitoring the heart rhythm. Administering potassium chloride (A) would exacerbate the hyperkalemia.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: "My stomach feels bloated and I've been vomiting constantly," says the patient post-op day 2 after abdominal surgery.

choices.text: Document the findings and reassess in an hour.
Assess for bowel sounds and abdominal distention.

questions.rationale: Post-operative nausea and vomiting (PONV), and bloating are common, but persistent vomiting and bloating suggest possible post-operative complications such as ileus or bowel obstruction.  A thorough abdominal assessment including auscultation for bowel sounds and palpation for distention (C) is crucial to determine the cause and appropriate intervention.  While administering antiemetics (B) may be necessary, a comprehensive assessment is paramount.  Ambulation (D) is beneficial post-op but secondary to addressing the potential complications. Simply documenting (A) without further assessment is inadequate.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: For a client with Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), which findings indicate effective treatment?
questions.rationale: Effective treatment of DKA is indicated by resolution of ketoacidosis, evidenced by decreasing blood glucose (A), increasing serum bicarbonate (B) which corrects acidosis, and resolution of Kussmaul respirations (D), a compensatory mechanism for metabolic acidosis.  Increased urine output (E) reflects improved hydration and renal perfusion.  Decreasing serum potassium (C) might be seen initially due to fluid shifts and insulin administration, but this is not necessarily an indicator of effective treatment, as potassium levels need to be monitored carefully and replaced as needed. Decreasing blood pressure (F) may indicate dehydration or other complications.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Potassium level is 5.8 mEq/L. Which assessment findings would the nurse expect in an elderly patient with hyperkalemia? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia (high potassium) can cause muscle weakness (A) and cardiac dysrhythmias (B) due to its effect on the heart's electrical conduction. Increased thirst (C) and polyuria (F) are more associated with hypernatremia (high sodium). Hypotension (D) is not a direct effect of hyperkalemia. Nausea and vomiting (E) are less common symptoms directly caused by hyperkalemia.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: "My chest feels tight, and I can't catch my breath," says a patient experiencing a panic attack.  What is the nurse's priority intervention?

choices.text: Immediately place the patient in seclusion.
Reassure the patient that the symptoms will pass.

questions.rationale: While medication (A) may be indicated, initially focusing on calming the patient through breathing exercises (B) is the priority.  Seclusion (C) is inappropriate and could escalate anxiety. Reassurance (D) is helpful but less effective than immediate breathing techniques.  The goal is to reduce the physiological symptoms of the panic attack first.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: For a client with Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), which interventions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: DKA requires prompt insulin administration (A) to lower blood glucose. Frequent blood glucose monitoring (B) is essential to guide insulin therapy.  Potassium is often depleted in DKA, requiring replacement (E). Fluid replacement is crucial (not restriction, C) because of dehydration.  Hypokalemia (F) is a common complication of DKA; however, monitoring for it isn't as critical as actively replacing potassium.  Encouraging oral fluids (D) is contraindicated until fluid replacement is initiated via IV.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a new ileostomy reports persistent skin irritation around the stoma. What is the most likely cause?
questions.rationale: Skin irritation is most commonly caused by improper ostomy pouch application, leading to leakage of stool and digestive enzymes.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which individual is at highest risk for developing influenza?

choices.text: A 25-year-old with a mild allergy to penicillin

questions.rationale: Infants under 6 months are at high risk of severe complications from influenza due to an underdeveloped immune system.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which nursing interventions are appropriate for a patient experiencing chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV)? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Restrict dietary intake during severe nausea.

questions.rationale: Anti-emetics (A) are essential for managing CINV.  Restricting intake (C) during severe nausea prevents further vomiting. Bland, low-fat foods (D) are better tolerated. Assessing for dehydration (E) is crucial. Encouraging ambulation (F) may not be appropriate if severe nausea and vomiting are present.  Clear liquids (B) may worsen nausea for some patients.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is prescribed digoxin. Which assessment finding requires immediate intervention?

choices.text: Heart rate of 60 beats per minute.

questions.rationale: Hypokalemia (low potassium) increases the risk of digoxin toxicity.  A potassium level of 3.0 mEq/L is significantly low and requires immediate intervention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which finding indicates a potential complication following a colonoscopy?
questions.rationale: Rectal bleeding following a colonoscopy suggests a potential perforation or other complication that requires immediate investigation.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a new diagnosis of COPD presents with a PaO2 of 55 mmHg and PaCO2 of 60 mmHg. Select all appropriate nursing interventions.

choices.text: Initiate oxygen therapy at 2 L/min via nasal cannula.
Monitor for signs of respiratory distress.
Position the patient in high-Fowler's position.
Restrict fluids to prevent fluid overload.

questions.rationale: A PaO2 of 55 mmHg and PaCO2 of 60 mmHg indicate hypoxemia and hypercapnia, characteristic of worsening COPD.  B, C, D, and E are all appropriate interventions to address these issues. Pursed-lip breathing helps slow expiration and improve gas exchange (B). Bronchodilators open airways, improving ventilation (C). Monitoring for respiratory distress is crucial to catch worsening hypoxemia (D). High-Fowler's facilitates breathing by reducing diaphragmatic pressure (E).  A is incorrect; high oxygen flow in COPD patients with chronic hypercapnia can suppress their respiratory drive. F is incorrect; fluid restriction is not typically indicated in COPD unless there is evidence of heart failure.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Potassium level is 5.8 mEq/L. Which of the following nursing interventions is MOST important?
questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia (potassium level >5.0 mEq/L) can cause life-threatening cardiac dysrhythmias.  Therefore, continuous cardiac monitoring (A) is the priority. While B, C, and D are also appropriate interventions for managing hyperkalemia, they are secondary to monitoring the patient's cardiac status and preventing potential lethal arrhythmias.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient diagnosed with schizophrenia states, "The voices are telling me to hurt myself."  What is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Document the patient's statement in the chart.
Assess the patient's risk for self-harm.
Encourage the patient to participate in group therapy.

questions.rationale: The patient's statement indicates an immediate risk of self-harm. The nurse's priority is to assess the patient's risk (B), including the patient's plan, means, and intent.  While A, C, and D are important aspects of care, they are secondary to ensuring the patient's safety.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving morphine sulfate for post-operative pain. Which assessment finding requires immediate intervention?

choices.text: Respiratory rate of 10 breaths per minute.
Heart rate of 80 beats per minute.
Pain rated 3/10 on a numerical scale.

questions.rationale: A respiratory rate of 10 breaths per minute indicates respiratory depression, a serious side effect of opioid analgesics like morphine.  Immediate intervention is required, such as administering naloxone or reducing the morphine dose.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse just completed a dressing change on a patient with a draining wound.  What is the appropriate disposal method for the soiled dressing?

choices.text: Discard in a regular trash can.
Discard in a biohazard waste container.

questions.rationale: Soiled dressings from a draining wound are considered contaminated and should be discarded in a biohazard waste container (B) to prevent the spread of infection.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with pneumonia is receiving oxygen therapy.  Which assessment finding suggests the need for increased oxygen support?

choices.text: Clear lung sounds in the apices.

questions.rationale: An oxygen saturation of 88% on 2L/min (C) indicates inadequate oxygenation, necessitating an increase in oxygen flow rate.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a new ileostomy reports leakage around the stoma. Select all appropriate nursing interventions.

choices.text: Assess the stoma for signs of ischemia.
Apply a skin barrier paste around the stoma.
Teach the patient proper ostomy care.
Check the ostomy appliance for proper fit.
Increase fluid intake to prevent dehydration.

questions.rationale: Leakage around a new ileostomy requires a multifaceted approach. Assessing for ischemia (A) is crucial to rule out circulatory compromise.  A skin barrier paste (C) protects the peristomal skin.  Proper ostomy care education (D) is essential for long-term management.  Checking the appliance fit (E) ensures a good seal. F is incorrect; while hydration is important for ostomy patients, increasing fluids won't directly address the leakage.  B is not directly relevant to immediate leakage management; it's more a part of ongoing stoma assessment.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which assessment findings would the nurse expect in a patient experiencing an anaphylactic reaction following a transfusion reaction?
questions.rationale: Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction.  Hypotension (A), bronchospasm (B), urticaria (hives, C), tachycardia (D), and angioedema (F) are all common manifestations. Increased temperature (E) is not typically associated with anaphylaxis.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A child presents with a barking cough and inspiratory stridor. What is the likely diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Croup is characterized by a barking cough and inspiratory stridor due to inflammation of the upper airway.  Bronchitis, pneumonia, and asthma have different presentations.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with COPD presents with SpO2 86% on room air, respiratory rate 32 breaths/minute, and increased work of breathing. Select all appropriate nursing interventions.

choices.text: Administer oxygen at 2L/min via nasal cannula.
Auscultate lung sounds for wheezes and crackles.
Prepare for intubation and mechanical ventilation.
Restrict fluids to prevent fluid overload.

questions.rationale: COPD patients are often oxygen sensitive, and high oxygen flow rates can suppress their hypoxic drive.  Pursed-lip breathing and bronchodilators help improve ventilation.  Lung sound assessment guides treatment.  Intubation is a last resort for severe respiratory distress.  Fluid restriction is not routinely indicated in COPD.  Option A risks oxygen toxicity; Option E is premature unless the patient deteriorates significantly; Option F is inappropriate unless there is specific indication of fluid overload.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A 2-year-old child presents with a temperature of 102°F (39°C) and is refusing to eat.

choices.text: Force the child to eat to maintain nutritional intake.
Offer small amounts of cool fluids frequently.
Give the child a warm bath to lower the temperature.

questions.rationale: Fever in young children requires careful monitoring and management. Forcing a child to eat can be counterproductive. Aspirin should be avoided in children due to the risk of Reye's syndrome. A warm bath is not recommended for fever reduction. Offering cool fluids helps maintain hydration, which is crucial.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient diagnosed with schizophrenia states, "The voices are telling me to hurt myself."

choices.text: Reassure the patient that the voices are not real.
Leave the patient alone to reduce stimulation.
Document the statement and assess the patient's risk for self-harm.
Engage the patient in a distracting activity.

questions.rationale: Auditory hallucinations are a common symptom of schizophrenia. The nurse's priority is to assess the patient's risk for self-harm and implement safety precautions.  Dismissing the hallucinations (A) is invalidating. Leaving the patient alone (B) is unsafe.  Distraction (D) may be helpful but is secondary to risk assessment.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which of the following are common age-related changes that can affect medication absorption in older adults?
questions.rationale: Decreased gastric acid secretion impairs the absorption of some medications. Reduced hepatic blood flow slows metabolism. Decreased renal function diminishes excretion, leading to drug accumulation. Increased intestinal motility (B) and reduced muscle mass (F) do not directly affect absorption.  Body water percentage (E) decreases with age, not increases.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Potassium level is 5.8 mEq/L.  Which of the following is the priority nursing intervention?

choices.text: Restrict fluids to dilute the potassium.

questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia (elevated potassium) can cause life-threatening cardiac dysrhythmias.  Cardiac monitoring is the priority. Options A and B would worsen the hyperkalemia. Fluid restriction (D) is not the primary intervention for hyperkalemia.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which community health nursing intervention is most effective in preventing the spread of influenza?

choices.text: Providing individual counseling on hygiene practices.
Implementing a community-wide influenza vaccination campaign.
Conducting educational sessions on handwashing techniques.

questions.rationale: A community-wide vaccination campaign provides the broadest protection against influenza compared to individual-level interventions.  While individual counseling, pamphlets and handwashing education are helpful, vaccination is the most effective population-level strategy.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a new diagnosis of multiple sclerosis (MS) is being discharged.  What teaching points should the nurse include?

choices.text: Importance of adhering to a strict low-sodium diet.
Methods for managing bowel and bladder dysfunction.

questions.rationale: Exercise, fatigue management, fall prevention, and bowel/bladder management are all crucial aspects of MS care.  A strict low-sodium diet is not universally required in MS.  While stress reduction is beneficial, complete avoidance is unrealistic and impractical.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with COPD presents with increased shortness of breath, wheezing, and a SpO2 of 86% on room air.  Select all appropriate nursing interventions.

choices.text: Administer oxygen at 10L via nasal cannula.
Place the patient in high-Fowler's position.
Prepare for intubation and mechanical ventilation.

questions.rationale: COPD patients are at risk for hypoxemia and hypercapnia.  While supplemental oxygen is necessary (A), high flow rates can worsen respiratory drive. Option A is incorrect because it uses too high an oxygen flow.  Pursed-lip breathing (B), assessing lung sounds (C), administering bronchodilators (D), and positioning (E) are all appropriate interventions to improve oxygenation and reduce dyspnea.  Intubation (F) may be necessary if the patient's condition deteriorates significantly but is not the immediate priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A 2-year-old child is brought to the clinic with a fever of 102°F (39°C) and a persistent cough. Which assessment finding requires immediate attention?

choices.text: The child is irritable and crying.
The child's nasal passages are congested.
The child is exhibiting signs of respiratory distress (retractions, nasal flaring).
The child's cough is productive of thick mucus.

questions.rationale: Respiratory distress (C) in a young child is a serious sign that warrants immediate attention, as it indicates the child is struggling to breathe.  Irritability (A), nasal congestion (B), and a productive cough (D) are common symptoms of upper respiratory infections but do not pose the same level of immediate risk as respiratory distress.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: "My chest feels tight, and I can't breathe," says a patient experiencing a panic attack. What nursing interventions are appropriate?

choices.text: Stay with the patient and offer reassurance.
Leave the patient alone to regain composure.

questions.rationale: During a panic attack, patients experience intense anxiety and fear, often with somatic manifestations like chest tightness and shortness of breath.  Reassurance (B) and relaxation techniques (C), such as deep breathing, can help manage the symptoms. Administering anxiolytics (D) as prescribed can also help alleviate symptoms. Restraining (E) is inappropriate and would escalate the situation. Leaving the patient (F) alone is also not appropriate; patients experiencing panic attacks require support and reassurance. Oxygen (A) may be helpful if there are respiratory symptoms beyond the panic attack.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Potassium level is 5.8 mEq/L. What is the nurse's priority concern?
questions.rationale: A potassium level of 5.8 mEq/L is significantly elevated, indicating hyperkalemia.  Hyperkalemia can lead to life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias. Hypokalemia, hyponatremia, and hypernatremia are not reflected by this potassium level.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A 2-year-old child presents with a barking cough and stridor.  What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.rationale: The classic symptoms of croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) are a barking cough and stridor.  While the other options can present with respiratory symptoms, this combination is most indicative of croup.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: After administering furosemide (Lasix), which assessment finding requires immediate intervention?
questions.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension is a significant adverse effect of furosemide, a potent loop diuretic. A sudden drop in blood pressure upon standing can lead to falls and injury. While the other options are potential side effects, orthostatic hypotension is a more immediate concern requiring intervention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: "My chest feels tight, and I can't breathe," the patient says. What is the nurse's priority assessment?

choices.text: Check for a history of anxiety disorders.

questions.rationale: The patient's statement indicates potential respiratory distress, requiring immediate assessment of respiratory status (rate, depth, effort, oxygen saturation). While the other options are relevant to the patient's overall care, the immediate priority is to address the reported breathing difficulty.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with severe dehydration exhibits tenting of the skin. This is an example of:
questions.rationale: Tenting of the skin indicates decreased skin turgor, which is a sign of dehydration.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Potassium level is 5.8 mEq/L in a 10-year-old child. Which is the priority nursing assessment?
questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia (elevated potassium) poses a significant risk of cardiac dysrhythmias, which can be life-threatening.  Therefore, assessing the cardiac rhythm (A) is the priority.  While other assessments (B, C, D) are relevant to overall health, cardiac monitoring takes precedence in this situation.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: "My chest feels tight, and I can't catch my breath," states a patient with panic disorder. What is the nurse's best initial response?

choices.text: "Let's talk about what triggered this anxiety."
"I'll get you medication to help calm you down."

questions.rationale: The patient's statement indicates acute distress.  The nurse should first focus on immediate symptom management by promoting relaxation techniques like slow, deep breaths (B).  While exploring triggers (A) is important, it's not the immediate priority. Reassurance (C) without addressing the immediate physiological symptoms is insufficient.  Medication (D) may be necessary, but it is not the first intervention.  The nurse's focus is to provide immediate comfort and reduce the patient's anxiety level.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: After administering furosemide, which assessment finding requires immediate intervention?
questions.rationale: Furosemide is a potent diuretic, so increased urine output (A) is expected.  Hypokalemia (C) is a potential side effect requiring monitoring but is not an immediate threat compared to a significant drop in blood pressure (B), which can indicate hypovolemic shock.  Orthostatic hypotension (D) is also a side effect, but a dramatic decrease in blood pressure is more serious and needs immediate attention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: For a client with Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), which lab value requires immediate action by the nurse?
questions.rationale: While all the options represent abnormalities in DKA, the arterial pH of 7.20 (D) indicates severe acidosis, which is a life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention.  The other values (A, B, C) are also concerning but less immediately life-threatening.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which are potential complications of a large bowel obstruction? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: A large bowel obstruction leads to fluid and electrolyte loss (A, B), potentially causing dehydration and electrolyte imbalances (A, B).  The build-up of pressure can lead to bowel perforation (C). If perforation occurs, bacterial entry into the peritoneal cavity can lead to sepsis (F). Metabolic acidosis (D) is more associated with small bowel obstruction. Hypokalemia (E) is a possible electrolyte imbalance, but other imbalances are more likely initially.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse just completed administering a dose of intravenous potassium chloride. What is the most important immediate nursing action?

choices.text: Monitor the IV site for infiltration.

questions.rationale: Potassium chloride is a highly irritating medication that can cause phlebitis or infiltration if administered incorrectly.  Monitoring the IV site (D) for any signs of infiltration is the most important immediate action. While monitoring urine output (A), assessing for hyperkalemia (B), and checking blood pressure (C) are important, they are secondary to preventing immediate harm from IV infiltration.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which is the best way to assess a community's health needs?
questions.rationale: A comprehensive community health assessment (B) uses multiple methods to gather information about the community's health status, needs, and resources.  While other options (A, C, D) provide some information, a formal community health assessment provides the most thorough and systematic approach.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is prescribed warfarin (Coumadin).  What lab value should the nurse monitor?
questions.rationale: INR is used to monitor the effectiveness of warfarin therapy.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient receiving chemotherapy reports severe mouth sores. What is the priority nursing intervention?

choices.text: Encourage frequent mouth rinsing with salt water.

questions.rationale: Mucositis (mouth sores) from chemotherapy increases the risk of infection.  Assessing for signs of infection (C) is the priority. While pain management (A), mouth care (B), and dietary changes (D) are important supportive measures, preventing infection takes precedence.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which nursing interventions are appropriate for a patient experiencing a hypertensive crisis? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Place the patient in a supine position.
Restrain the patient to prevent injury.

questions.rationale: A hypertensive crisis necessitates close monitoring for neurological changes (A) (e.g., altered mental status, seizures), administration of prescribed antihypertensive medications (B), and assessment for organ damage (C) (e.g., renal failure, stroke).  Placing the patient supine (D) may not be necessary and could worsen cerebral perfusion in some cases.  Restraining (E) is not appropriate unless the patient poses a danger to themselves or others. Fluid restriction (F) might be indicated depending on the cause and severity, but is not a primary intervention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a nasogastric (NG) tube suddenly becomes dyspneic. What is the priority nursing action?
questions.rationale: Sudden dyspnea with an NG tube in place suggests the tube may have migrated into the lungs (aspiration).  Checking NG tube placement (A) is the priority to determine if this is the cause of the dyspnea.  While assessing oxygen saturation (B) and suctioning (D) are important, they are secondary to confirming NG tube placement.  Irrigating (C) should be done only if the placement is confirmed.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A 2-year-old child presents with a fever of 102°F (38.9°C) and a barking cough. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.rationale: The symptoms of fever, barking cough, and age suggest croup (C), a viral infection of the upper airway. Pneumonia (A) usually presents with other respiratory symptoms. Bronchiolitis (B) is more common in infants.  Epiglottitis (D) is a medical emergency with severe respiratory distress and is less likely to present with a barking cough.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is prescribed warfarin.  The nurse notes an INR of 5.0. What is the priority nursing action?

choices.text: Hold the next dose of warfarin.

questions.rationale: An INR of 5.0 is significantly elevated, indicating a high risk of bleeding.  The priority is to hold the next dose of warfarin (B) to prevent further anticoagulation. Vitamin K (A) may be administered, but holding the warfarin is the immediate action. Monitoring for bleeding (C) is important, but it is secondary to preventing further anticoagulation.  Assessing for thrombosis (D) is not the immediate concern when the INR is significantly elevated.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: An elderly patient is experiencing confusion and disorientation. What is the priority assessment?
questions.rationale: Confusion and disorientation in the elderly can be caused by numerous factors, including medication side effects.  Therefore, obtaining a thorough medication history (A) is the priority assessment.  While other factors (B, C, D) can also contribute to confusion, medications are a frequent cause and need to be evaluated first.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which type of wound dressing is best for a deep wound with significant drainage?
questions.rationale: Alginate dressings (B) are highly absorbent and ideal for wounds with copious drainage.  Hydrocolloids (A) are suitable for less draining wounds. Film dressings (C) are impermeable and unsuitable for wounds with significant drainage. Gauze (D) is absorbent but can adhere to the wound bed, causing pain on removal.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which findings suggest hypoxemia? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Hypoxemia leads to increased respiratory rate (A) (tachypnea) as the body tries to compensate.  Cyanosis (C) is a visible sign of decreased oxygenation.  Restlessness (D) and confusion (E) are early neurological signs of hypoxemia.  Hypotension (F) is not a direct sign of hypoxemia, although it can be a consequence of severe hypoxemia.  Bradycardia (B) is not typical; tachycardia is more common.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with hypothyroidism reports fatigue and weight gain.  Which lab result confirms the diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Hypothyroidism is characterized by decreased thyroid hormone production.  The pituitary gland compensates by releasing more TSH.  Therefore, an elevated TSH (A) is consistent with hypothyroidism. Decreased TSH (B) would indicate problems with the pituitary gland, not the thyroid. Elevated T3 and T4 (C) would indicate hyperthyroidism.  Decreased T3 and T4 (D) is expected in hypothyroidism, but the elevated TSH is the confirming factor.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient receiving radiation therapy reports severe skin reactions at the treatment site. Which nursing intervention is MOST important?

choices.text: Apply a moisturizing lotion to the area.
Protect the area from sun exposure.
Avoid using harsh soaps or detergents.

questions.rationale: Radiation-induced skin reactions make the skin vulnerable to further damage, especially from sun exposure.  Protecting the area from sun exposure (C) is the MOST important to prevent further injury and promote healing.  While pain management (A), moisturizing (B), and avoiding harsh soaps (D) are helpful, sun protection is paramount.

questions.title: Nursing Question
